edges.description: Reduced MCAD activity blocks dehydrogenation of C4-C12 acyl-CoA substrates.
Impaired fatty acid oxidation prevents compensatory ketogenesis during fasting.
Blocked MCAD-dependent oxidation causes medium-chain fatty acid and conjugate accumulation.
Fasting energy failure lowers circulating glucose.
Impaired hepatic fatty acid oxidation leaves ketone production inappropriately low.
Low glucose and low ketone production are the defining acute metabolic phenotype.
Acute metabolic decompensation can injure the liver and raise circulating transaminases.
Acute decompensation can include hyperammonemia as a metabolic crisis feature.
Acute MCAD decompensation can include hepatomegaly as part of the hepatic crisis phenotype.
intermediate_mechanisms: acute hepatic energy failure during fasting or illness

edges.description: Uncontrolled metabolic decompensation can leave chronic skeletal-muscle sequelae.
Acute metabolic decompensation commonly includes vomiting.
Acute energy failure manifests as lethargy during metabolic decompensation.
Severe hypoglycemia can progress to symptomatic seizures.
Acute hypoketotic hypoglycemia and energy failure can produce encephalopathy.
Severe acute decompensation can progress to coma.
Acute MCAD decompensation may resemble Reye syndrome, historically leading to diagnostic confusion with Reye's syndrome or sudden infant death.
Untreated acute metabolic crises can be fatal.
Alternative oxidation produces nonketotic dicarboxylic aciduria.
Accumulated medium-chain acyl groups are excreted as octanoylcarnitine.
Medium-chain acyl-CoA accumulation drives glycine conjugate excretion.
Accumulated medium-chain intermediates impair mitochondrial respiration.

Developmental disability is reported as a long-term outcome in MCAD deficiency, but the disease-specific route from acute energy failure, secondary OXPHOS dysfunction, or other modifiers to developmental delay is not fully resolved.

Aphasia or language involvement is listed among manifestations requiring standard treatment in MCAD deficiency; the causal path is modeled conservatively because the specific intermediary mechanism is unresolved.
Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder is a reported long-term neurobehavioral association in MCAD deficiency, but the mechanism is not resolved.
Decreased blood glucose reports the fasting energy-failure branch caused by inadequate fatty-acid-derived ketone support.
Low blood glucose is the biochemical readout of the hypoglycemic component of the defining acute phenotype.
Inappropriately low ketone bodies report impaired hepatic ketogenesis downstream of the medium-chain FAO block.
Low ketone production is the biochemical readout of the hypoketotic component of MCAD-associated hypoglycemia.
Elevated C8 acylcarnitine reports impaired oxidation of medium-chain acyl-CoA substrates on newborn screening.
Increased C8 acylcarnitine reflects accumulated medium-chain acyl groups diverted into carnitine conjugates.
Increased urinary dicarboxylic acids report alternative omega-oxidation and excretion of medium-chain metabolites during acute episodes.
Urinary octanoylcarnitine reports excretion of accumulated medium-chain acyl groups as carnitine conjugates.
Increased urinary acylglycines report glycine conjugation and excretion of accumulated medium-chain acyl-CoA metabolites.

nodes.description: Biallelic pathogenic variants in ACADM reduce medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase activity in the mitochondrial matrix. Many disease-associated missense variants destabilize the MCAD protein, causing misfolding and loss of catalytic function.

evidence.reference_title: Medium chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency human genome epidemiology review.
evidence.snippet: Medium chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase (MCAD) is a tetrameric flavoprotein essential for the beta-oxidation of medium chain fatty acids. MCAD deficiency (MCADD) is an inherited error of fatty acid metabolism.

evidence.reference_title: Protein misfolding is the molecular mechanism underlying MCADD identified in newborn screening.
evidence.snippet: Taken together, our results substantiate the hypothesis of protein misfolding with loss-of-function being the common molecular basis in MCADD.

evidence.reference_title: Medium chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency human genome epidemiology review.
evidence.snippet: One variant of the MCAD gene, G985A, a point mutation causing a change from lysine to glutamate at position 304 (K304E) in the mature MCAD protein, has been found in 90% of the alleles in MCADD patients identified retrospectively.

evidence.reference_title: Medium chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency human genome epidemiology review.
evidence.snippet: Of patients clinically diagnosed with MCADD, 81% who have been identified retrospectively are homozygous for K304E, and 18% are compound heterozygotes for K304E.

evidence.reference_title: Medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase (MCAD) deficiency: the prevalent mutation G985 (K304E) is subject to a strong founder effect from northwestern Europe.
evidence.snippet: Approximately 90% of the disease-causing alleles in diagnosed patients are due to a single base mutation, an A (adenine) to G (guanine) transition at position 985 of MCAD cDNA (G985).

evidence.reference_title: Regional differences in the frequency of the c.985A>G ACADM mutation: findings from a meta-regression of genotyping and screening studies.
evidence.snippet: The proportion of c.985A>G homozygotes was highest in Western Europe with 4.1 (95%CI: 2.8-5.6) per 100,000 individuals, then the New World (3.2, 95%CI: 2.0-4.7), Southern (1.2, 95%CI: 0.6-2.0) and Eastern European regions (0.9, 95%CI: 0.5-1.7).

evidence.reference_title: ACADM / medium chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: ACADM | HGNC:89 | medium chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency | MONDO:0008721 | AR | Definitive

nodes.description: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder is a neurobehavioral association for which GeneReviews recommends standard treatment in affected individuals.

evidence.reference_title: Medium-Chain Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Standard treatment for developmental delay / aphasia, attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, and muscle weakness.

nodes.description: Alcohol, especially acute intoxication or binge drinking, should be avoided because it can elicit metabolic decompensation.

evidence.reference_title: Medium-Chain Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: alcohol consumption, in particular acute alcohol intoxication (e.g., binge drinking), which can elicit metabolic decompensation

nodes.description: Aspirin is listed by GeneReviews among agents or circumstances to avoid in MCAD deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Medium-Chain Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Agents/circumstances to avoid: Hypoglycemia; infant formulas, coconut oil, and other manufactured foods containing medium-chain triglycerides as the primary source of fat; popular high-fat/low-carbohydrate diets; alcohol consumption, in particular acute alcohol intoxication (e.g., binge drinking), which can elicit metabolic decompensation; aspirin.

nodes.description: Frequent feeding and avoidance of prolonged fasting is the cornerstone of management. Infants should not fast more than 4-6 hours; older children can tolerate longer intervals.

evidence.reference_title: Newborn screening for medium chain acyl CoA dehydrogenase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: The basic treatment is dietary: avoid fasting and ensure a high carbohydrate intake during any illness.

evidence.reference_title: Medium chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency human genome epidemiology review.
evidence.snippet: For those diagnosed, long-term management of the disease includes preventing stress caused by fasting and maintaining a high-carbohydrate, reduced-fat diet, and carnitine supplementation.

nodes.description: Avoid infant formulas, coconut oil, manufactured foods using medium-chain triglycerides as the primary fat source, and popular high-fat or low-carbohydrate diets.

evidence.reference_title: Medium-Chain Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Agents/circumstances to avoid: Hypoglycemia; infant formulas, coconut oil, and other manufactured foods containing medium-chain triglycerides as the primary source of fat; popular high-fat/low-carbohydrate diets; alcohol consumption, in particular acute alcohol intoxication (e.g., binge drinking), which can elicit metabolic decompensation; aspirin.

evidence.reference_title: Medium chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency human genome epidemiology review.
evidence.snippet: Clinical data on the probability of clinical disease indicates that MCADD patients are at risk for the following outcomes: hypoglycemia, vomiting, lethargy, encephalopathy, respiratory arrest, hepatomegaly, seizures, apnea, cardiac arrest, coma, and sudden and unexpected death.

readouts.interpretation: Decreased blood glucose reports the fasting energy-failure branch caused by inadequate fatty-acid-derived ketone support.
Low blood glucose is the biochemical readout of the hypoglycemic component of the defining acute phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of the Mitochondrial Fatty Acid β-Oxidation Protein Medium-Chain Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Disrupts Oxidative Phosphorylation Protein Complex Stability and Function.
evidence.snippet: Analysis of her dried blood spot at 5 days of age revealed C8-acylcarnitine levels of 0.44 nmol/mL (normal range < 0.3) and a C8/C10 acylcarnitine ratio of 1.81 (normal range < 1.4).

readouts.interpretation: Elevated C8 acylcarnitine reports impaired oxidation of medium-chain acyl-CoA substrates on newborn screening.
Increased C8 acylcarnitine reflects accumulated medium-chain acyl groups diverted into carnitine conjugates.

nodes.description: Individuals with uncontrolled metabolic decompensation can develop chronic myopathy as a longer-term sequela.

evidence.reference_title: Medium-Chain Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Individuals with MCAD deficiency who have experienced the effects of uncontrolled metabolic decompensation are also at risk for chronic myopathy.

nodes.description: Severe untreated metabolic decompensation can progress to coma.

evidence.reference_title: Medium chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency human genome epidemiology review.
evidence.snippet: Clinical data on the probability of clinical disease indicates that MCADD patients are at risk for the following outcomes: hypoglycemia, vomiting, lethargy, encephalopathy, respiratory arrest, hepatomegaly, seizures, apnea, cardiac arrest, coma, and sudden and unexpected death.

nodes.description: Acute MCAD metabolic decompensation can cause elevated liver transaminases, reflecting hepatic stress or injury during crisis episodes.

evidence.reference_title: Medium-Chain Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Metabolic decompensation during these episodes can result in elevated liver transaminases and hyperammonemia.

nodes.description: During illness or metabolic stress, intravenous glucose (10% dextrose) should be administered promptly to prevent hypoglycemia.

evidence.reference_title: Medium chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency human genome epidemiology review.
evidence.snippet: Acute attacks must be treated immediately with appropriate intravenous doses of glucose.

nodes.description: Acute metabolic crises can cause altered mental status and encephalopathy, particularly when severe hypoketotic hypoglycemia is untreated.

evidence.reference_title: Medium chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency human genome epidemiology review.
evidence.snippet: Clinical data on the probability of clinical disease indicates that MCADD patients are at risk for the following outcomes: hypoglycemia, vomiting, lethargy, encephalopathy, respiratory arrest, hepatomegaly, seizures, apnea, cardiac arrest, coma, and sudden and unexpected death.

nodes.description: Speech-language involvement, represented in GeneReviews as aphasia, can require standard supportive treatment after clinically significant MCAD disease expression.

evidence.reference_title: Medium-Chain Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Standard treatment for developmental delay / aphasia, attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, and muscle weakness.

nodes.description: Developmental delay can require standard supportive treatment in MCAD deficiency, particularly in individuals affected by uncontrolled metabolic crises before diagnosis or adequate preventive management.

evidence.reference_title: Medium-Chain Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Standard treatment for developmental delay / aphasia, attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, and muscle weakness.

nodes.description: Fatty infiltration of the liver with hepatomegaly during metabolic crises.

evidence.reference_title: Medium chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency human genome epidemiology review.
evidence.snippet: Clinical data on the probability of clinical disease indicates that MCADD patients are at risk for the following outcomes: hypoglycemia, vomiting, lethargy, encephalopathy, respiratory arrest, hepatomegaly, seizures, apnea, cardiac arrest, coma, and sudden and unexpected death.

nodes.description: Hyperammonemia can occur during acute MCAD metabolic decompensation.

evidence.reference_title: Medium-Chain Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Metabolic decompensation during these episodes can result in elevated liver transaminases and hyperammonemia.

nodes.description: Seizures may occur secondary to severe hypoglycemia.

evidence.reference_title: Medium chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency human genome epidemiology review.
evidence.snippet: Clinical data on the probability of clinical disease indicates that MCADD patients are at risk for the following outcomes: hypoglycemia, vomiting, lethargy, encephalopathy, respiratory arrest, hepatomegaly, seizures, apnea, cardiac arrest, coma, and sudden and unexpected death.

nodes.description: Low blood glucose with inappropriately low or absent ketones during fasting or illness. Classic presenting feature.

evidence.reference_title: Medium chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency human genome epidemiology review.
evidence.snippet: Clinical data on the probability of clinical disease indicates that MCADD patients are at risk for the following outcomes: hypoglycemia, vomiting, lethargy, encephalopathy, respiratory arrest, hepatomegaly, seizures, apnea, cardiac arrest, coma, and sudden and unexpected death.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of the Mitochondrial Fatty Acid β-Oxidation Protein Medium-Chain Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Disrupts Oxidative Phosphorylation Protein Complex Stability and Function.
evidence.snippet: Presentation is usually in childhood between the ages of three to 24 months, with patients asymptomatic until a period of prolonged fasting or a common illness triggers symptoms of hypoketotic hypoglycemia, vomiting and lethargy.

nodes.description: During fasting or intercurrent illness, hepatic glycogen stores become depleted and the liver normally relies on fatty acid oxidation to fuel ketone body production. MCAD deficiency blocks that compensatory response, producing low glucose with inappropriately low ketones and acute neurologic decompensation.

biological_processes: generation of precursor metabolites and energy

evidence.reference_title: Loss of the Mitochondrial Fatty Acid β-Oxidation Protein Medium-Chain Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Disrupts Oxidative Phosphorylation Protein Complex Stability and Function.
evidence.snippet: Presentation is usually in childhood between the ages of three to 24 months, with patients asymptomatic until a period of prolonged fasting or a common illness triggers symptoms of hypoketotic hypoglycemia, vomiting and lethargy.

nodes.description: Impaired mitochondrial oxidation of medium-chain fatty acyl-CoA substrates prevents normal fasting energy production. Reduced fatty acid oxidation limits acetyl-CoA supply for hepatic ketogenesis and diverts medium-chain substrates into alternative conjugated metabolites.

biological_processes: fatty acid beta-oxidation using acyl-CoA dehydrogenase

evidence.reference_title: Loss of the Mitochondrial Fatty Acid β-Oxidation Protein Medium-Chain Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Disrupts Oxidative Phosphorylation Protein Complex Stability and Function.
evidence.snippet: FAO is the primary biochemical pathway for fueling hepatic ketogenesis, a major source of energy once local glycogen stores have been depleted during times of fasting or high energy requirements.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of the Mitochondrial Fatty Acid β-Oxidation Protein Medium-Chain Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Disrupts Oxidative Phosphorylation Protein Complex Stability and Function.
evidence.snippet: Presentation is usually in childhood between the ages of three to 24 months, with patients asymptomatic until a period of prolonged fasting or a common illness triggers symptoms of hypoketotic hypoglycemia, vomiting and lethargy.

readouts.interpretation: Inappropriately low ketone bodies report impaired hepatic ketogenesis downstream of the medium-chain FAO block.
Low ketone production is the biochemical readout of the hypoketotic component of MCAD-associated hypoglycemia.

nodes.description: Progressive lethargy during metabolic decompensation, may progress to coma.

evidence.reference_title: Medium chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency human genome epidemiology review.
evidence.snippet: Clinical data on the probability of clinical disease indicates that MCADD patients are at risk for the following outcomes: hypoglycemia, vomiting, lethargy, encephalopathy, respiratory arrest, hepatomegaly, seizures, apnea, cardiac arrest, coma, and sudden and unexpected death.

nodes.description: Acute encephalopathy with fatty liver resembling Reye syndrome may occur during metabolic crises.

evidence.reference_title: Medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency. Diagnosis by stable-isotope dilution measurement of urinary n-hexanoylglycine and 3-phenylpropionylglycine.
evidence.snippet: Medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase (MCAD) deficiency, one of the most common inherited metabolic disorders, is often mistaken for the sudden infant death syndrome or Reye's syndrome.

nodes.description: Loss of MCAD in patient fibroblasts and ACADM-targeted cells destabilizes OXPHOS complexes and supercomplexes, reduces mitochondrial oxygen consumption, and increases oxidative stress under respiratory stress. This secondary respiratory-chain dysfunction may contribute to disease pathology beyond the primary beta-oxidation block.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of the Mitochondrial Fatty Acid β-Oxidation Protein Medium-Chain Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Disrupts Oxidative Phosphorylation Protein Complex Stability and Function.
evidence.snippet: We found that mitochondrial oxygen consumption in MCAD-deficient patient fibroblasts and gene-targeted MCAD ‘knockout’ (KO) cells was reduced compared to controls.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of the Mitochondrial Fatty Acid β-Oxidation Protein Medium-Chain Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Disrupts Oxidative Phosphorylation Protein Complex Stability and Function.
evidence.snippet: We also found a reduction of the steady-state levels of OXPHOS complexes I, III and IV, as well as the OXPHOS supercomplex.

nodes.description: Prior to newborn screening, MCAD deficiency was a significant cause of sudden infant death, often triggered by intercurrent illness.

evidence.reference_title: Medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency. Diagnosis by stable-isotope dilution measurement of urinary n-hexanoylglycine and 3-phenylpropionylglycine.
evidence.snippet: Medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase (MCAD) deficiency, one of the most common inherited metabolic disorders, is often mistaken for the sudden infant death syndrome or Reye's syndrome.

nodes.description: Blocked medium-chain beta-oxidation causes accumulation and urinary excretion of medium-chain dicarboxylic acids, acylglycines, and acylcarnitines. Octanoic and decanoic acid intermediates may impair mitochondrial oxygen consumption and promote oxidative stress.

evidence.reference_title: Abnormal urinary excretion of unsaturated dicarboxylic acids in patients with medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Acute episodes are usually characterized biochemically by the appearance of nonketotic dicarboxylic aciduria. In addition, other abnormal metabolites, such as suberylglycine, n-hexanoylglycine, 3-phenylpropionylglycine, and octanoylcarnitine, are excreted in the urine.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of the Mitochondrial Fatty Acid β-Oxidation Protein Medium-Chain Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Disrupts Oxidative Phosphorylation Protein Complex Stability and Function.
evidence.snippet: MCAD deficiency not only disrupts this process, but also results in the accumulation of fatty acid intermediates such as octanoic and decanoic acids.

evidence.reference_title: Medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency. Diagnosis by stable-isotope dilution measurement of urinary n-hexanoylglycine and 3-phenylpropionylglycine.
evidence.snippet: The levels of urinary hexanoylglycine and phenylpropionylglycine were significantly increased in all samples from the patients with MCAD deficiency, clearly distinguishing them from both groups of controls.

readouts.interpretation: Increased urinary acylglycines report glycine conjugation and excretion of accumulated medium-chain acyl-CoA metabolites.

evidence.reference_title: Abnormal urinary excretion of unsaturated dicarboxylic acids in patients with medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Acute episodes are usually characterized biochemically by the appearance of nonketotic dicarboxylic aciduria.

readouts.interpretation: Increased urinary dicarboxylic acids report alternative omega-oxidation and excretion of medium-chain metabolites during acute episodes.

evidence.reference_title: Abnormal urinary excretion of unsaturated dicarboxylic acids in patients with medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: In addition, other abnormal metabolites, such as suberylglycine, n-hexanoylglycine, 3-phenylpropionylglycine, and octanoylcarnitine, are excreted in the urine.

readouts.interpretation: Urinary octanoylcarnitine reports excretion of accumulated medium-chain acyl groups as carnitine conjugates.

nodes.description: Vomiting is a common symptom during acute metabolic decompensation and can worsen catabolic stress.

evidence.reference_title: Medium chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency human genome epidemiology review.
evidence.snippet: Clinical data on the probability of clinical disease indicates that MCADD patients are at risk for the following outcomes: hypoglycemia, vomiting, lethargy, encephalopathy, respiratory arrest, hepatomegaly, seizures, apnea, cardiac arrest, coma, and sudden and unexpected death.
